case_name: Mills  v Oxford University Hospitals NHS Trust [2019] EWHC 936 (QB) (12 April 2019)

evidence_index.text: On 4 December 2012, Mr Mills underwent brain surgery at the John Radcliffe Hospital, specifically, a resection (otherwise known as de-bulking) of a left frontal glioma (i.e. a tumour).
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: He suffered a haemorrhage during the course of the operation which caused him to suffer a stroke in the left anterior cerebral artery territory.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: My overall conclusion is that the claim against the Trust (which accepts vicarious liability) based on lack of informed consent succeeds, whereas the claim based on clinical negligence fails.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: Mr Kirkpatrick was dismissive of the cosmetic advantage of having a smaller craniotomy. His view was that 'for operations involving the cranium, the vast majority of patients declare little regard for the size of the incision.'
evidence_index.speaker: Mr Peter Kirkpatrick

evidence_index.text: Mr Grundy, while acknowledging that the most important consideration for patients would be the outcome, said that he was regularly asked about cosmesis and that it was often a concern for patients.
evidence_index.speaker: Mr Paul Grundy

evidence_index.text: The use of a minimally invasive endoscopically-assisted open craniotomy technique to resect Mr Mills' glioma was not negligent.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: Mr Mills has not proved, on the balance of probabilities, that Mr Plaha performed the surgery negligently by migrating into the midline structures (or otherwise).
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Mr Plaha discussed three treatment options (surveillance, biopsy and resection), and the risks and benefits of each of them, with Mr Mills during the consultation in his clinic on 8 November 2012.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Mr Plaha breached his duty of care by (a) not offering a microscopically-assisted resection procedure as an alternative to a minimally invasive endoscopically-assisted resection and (b) not explaining the comparative risks and benefits of these alternative surgical techniques.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: In their joint report, Mr Kirkpatrick and Mr Grundy agreed that had the torrential bleeding occurred with a conventional technique the source of the bleeding would have been detectable and/or the bleeding could have been successfully controlled earlier.
evidence_index.speaker: Mr Kirkpatrick and Mr Grundy

evidence_index.text: I accept the joint view expressed by Mr Kirkpatrick and Mr Grundy that if such torrential bleeding had occurred using the conventional technique, it is probable that it would have been controlled successfully much earlier and Mr Mills probably would not have suffered a stroke.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

evidence_index.text: Accordingly, I find that the breach of duty was causative of the stroke that Mr Mills suffered.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: The increased risk that it would be difficulty to control bleeding if it occurred, of which Mr Mills should have been warned, was one which eventuated.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

evidence_index.text: For all the above reasons the claim based on alleged clinical negligence in the performance of the surgery fails; the claim succeeds on the basis of informed consent.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

patient_demographics.relevant_social_history: Firefighter, employed by the London Fire Brigade

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Mr Mills began experiencing headaches in early 2012, which worsened with bright lights and lying down. He was found to have high blood pressure during a routine health check and was admitted to Wexham Park Hospital with chest pains on 31 October 2012.

vital_signs.blood_pressure: High blood pressure noted during routine health check

focused_exam.neurological: No deficits noted, Glasgow Coma Scale 15

initial_state.initial_working_problem: Left frontal lobe lesion, possible low-grade glioma
critical_findings: Lesion in left frontal lobe identified on MRI

requestables.name: MRI Brain
requestables.description: Magnetic resonance imaging of the brain

reveal.result_summary: Identified a lesion in the left frontal lobe

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Mr Mills presents with headaches and chest pain, leading to hospital admission and imaging

timeline_phases.name: Neurosurgical Assessment
timeline_phases.description: Referral to MDT and consultation with neurosurgeon

key_events.event: MDT meeting and referral to Mr Plaha

decision_points.prompt: What surgical technique should be offered to Mr Mills?

options.clinical_reasoning: Standard technique with better control over bleeding
Novel technique with limited direct visualization

actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Believed to be a reasonable approach

expected_action_court.description: Microscopically-assisted resection should have been offered
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence established it as the safer option

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Endoscopic technique posed greater risk to vessels not in direct line of sight
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Microscopically-assisted technique should have been used

patient_outcome.description: Patient suffered a stroke due to torrential bleeding during surgery

key_findings: Breach of duty in failing to provide informed consent
Causation established - choice of technique contributed to stroke

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Patient would have chosen a different technique if fully informed

factual_timeline.event: Mr Mills admitted to Wexham Park Hospital
Surgery performed at John Radcliffe Hospital

allegations.allegation: Failure to offer standard surgical technique

court_findings.finding: Breach of duty in failing to offer microscopically-assisted resection
Defendant breached duty of care by not providing informed consent
